name: Ninhursag
description: Ninhursag, also known as Nintu, Ninmah, and Belet-Ili, stands as a paramount mother goddess within the Mesopotamian pantheon. Her name, meaning "Lady of the Mountain," alludes to her association with the highlands and the source of life. She is primarily revered as the goddess of fertility, childbirth, and nurturing. Ninhursag's role extends beyond simple procreation; she is considered the mother of all living things, including both humans and animals. She is seen as the divine midwife, overseeing births and ensuring the continuation of life. Her worship was particularly prominent in Sumerian cities like Adab, Kish, and Lagash, where temples were dedicated to her. In art, she is often depicted with the omega symbol, representing the womb, further emphasizing her role as the great mother. Her influence is deeply woven into the cultural fabric of ancient Mesopotamia, reflecting the society's profound respect for fertility and the life-giving forces of nature.
subtitle: Mother of the Gods

keyMyths.title: Enki and Ninhursag
keyMyths.description: This myth recounts Enki's insatiable desire and his subsequent impregnation of Ninhursag's daughters. Ninhursag, angered by Enki's actions and the resulting sickly state of his body, withdraws from the world, causing vegetation to wither and life to stagnate. Eventually, she relents and heals Enki, giving birth to eight deities to cure his ailing body parts. This story underscores Ninhursag's power over life and fertility, as well as the dire consequences of disrupting the natural order.
keyMyths.source: Black, J., Cunningham, G., Flückiger-Hawker, E., Robson, E., & Zólyomi, G. (2019). The Literature of Ancient Sumer.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Humans
keyMyths.description: In some versions of the creation myth, Ninhursag plays a pivotal role in the creation of humans. After the Igigi gods rebel against their labor, the Anunnaki decide to create humans as a substitute workforce. Ninhursag, as the divine mother, is tasked with molding the humans from clay, guided by Enki's wisdom. This myth highlights Ninhursag's creative power and her connection to the human race, establishing her as a benevolent figure responsible for humanity's existence.
keyMyths.source: Dalley, S. (1989). Myths from Mesopotamia: Creation, the Flood, Gilgamesh, and Others.

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Ninsun
keyMyths.description: Ninsun, the mother of Gilgamesh, is often associated with Ninhursag or considered an aspect of her. The birth of Ninsun is sometimes linked to Ninhursag's role as a mother goddess, emphasizing the divine lineage and exceptional qualities of Gilgamesh. This connection reinforces Ninhursag's overarching influence on the royal line and the heroic figures of Mesopotamian mythology. The tale underscores the importance of divine ancestry in shaping the destiny of kings and heroes.
keyMyths.source: George, A. (2003). The Epic of Gilgamesh: The Babylonian Epic Poem and Other Texts in Akkadian and Sumerian.

sources.author: Samuel Noah Kramer
